title: Schistosoma haematobium (Urogenital Schistosomiasis)
buzzwords: traveler from sub-Saharan Africa or Middle East
freshwater snail intermediate host
blood fluke trematode infection
skin penetration during freshwater contact
adults live in bladder venous plexus
terminal hematuria and dysuria
ova with terminal spine in urine
Schistosoma haematobium vesical plexus infection

title: Schistosoma Life Cycle
buzzwords: two-host parasitic cycle in freshwater
eggs released in human urine or feces
snail is obligate intermediate host
free-swimming larvae penetrate intact skin
miracidia hatch and infect snails
cercariae become schistosomula in host
adults pair in mesenteric or vesical venules
miracidia–snail–cercariae–schistosomula sequence

title: Katayama Fever (Acute Schistosomiasis)
buzzwords: non-immune traveler 2–12 weeks post-exposure
fever, urticaria, myalgia, dry cough
marked peripheral eosinophilia
hepatosplenomegaly with lymphadenopathy
type III hypersensitivity to migrating worms
self-limited febrile serum-sickness-like illness
rare transverse myelitis or cerebral disease
Katayama fever from early egg deposition

title: Chronic Urogenital Schistosomiasis & Bladder SCC
buzzwords: long-standing painless terminal hematuria
sterile pyuria with frequency and dysuria
granulomatous bladder wall fibrosis and calcification
hydroureter and obstructive uropathy on imaging
female genital disease causing infertility
egg deposition drives chronic inflammation
endemic Egypt risk for bladder malignancy
squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder

title: Praziquantel Treatment
buzzwords: first-line antihelminthic for all Schistosoma species
single oral dose for trematode infection
adjunctive steroids in acute hypersensitivity phase
may repeat dose weeks later for immature worms
increases tegument permeability to calcium ions
spastic paralysis and tegument disruption
exposes parasite antigens to host immunity
praziquantel for fluke and tapeworm infections
